name: Bosnian Pyramids Claim
description: The Bosnian Pyramids claim, popularized by Semir Osmanagić starting in 2005, posits that a cluster of hills near Visoko, Bosnia and Herzegovina, are actually ancient, human-made pyramids. Osmanagić alleges these structures, particularly Visočica hill (dubbed the 'Pyramid of the Sun'), are the largest and oldest pyramids on Earth, dating back approximately 12,000-30,000 years. He also claims the site exhibits unusual energy phenomena, including an energy beam emanating from the top of Visočica. The claims extend to other nearby hills, identified as the Pyramid of the Moon, Pyramid of the Dragon, Pyramid of Mother Earth, and Pyramid of Love. Proponents suggest the pyramids were built by an advanced, pre-historic civilization. Mainstream archaeology and geology overwhelmingly reject the Bosnian Pyramids claim. Scholars maintain that the hills are natural geological formations, specifically flatirons, with their shape resulting from differential erosion of sedimentary layers. Archaeological investigations at the site have revealed medieval artifacts, confirming human activity in the area but not supporting the existence of ancient pyramids. The scientific community criticizes Osmanagić's methods as pseudoarchaeology, lacking rigorous methodology, relying on selective interpretation of evidence, and lacking peer-reviewed publications. The 'energy beam' and other purported anomalous phenomena have not been scientifically verified and are dismissed as pseudoscientific claims.
subtitle: Alleged ancient pyramids in Bosnia.

claims.claim: Visočica hill is the 'Pyramid of the Sun', the world's largest and oldest pyramid.
claims.evidence: Regular shape, orientation to cardinal directions, presence of concrete-like material, alleged energy beam.
claims.criticism: Geological surveys show Visočica is a natural flatiron formation. The 'concrete' is a conglomerate of natural origin. The 'energy beam' lacks scientific evidence.

claims.claim: The Bosnian pyramids were built by an advanced, pre-historic civilization.
claims.evidence: Alleged tunnels and underground chambers, advanced construction techniques.
claims.criticism: The 'tunnels' are natural cave systems or medieval mines. No evidence supports advanced construction techniques or a pre-historic civilization capable of building pyramids.

claims.claim: The pyramids emit measurable energy fields and possess healing properties.
claims.evidence: Reported positive effects on health, measurements of electromagnetic fields.
claims.criticism: Such claims are anecdotal and lack scientific validation. Electromagnetic fields are naturally occurring and do not prove the existence of pyramids or healing properties.

keyFigures.name: Semir Osmanagić
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the Bosnian Pyramids claim and lead researcher.

keyFigures.name: Enver Imamović
keyFigures.role: Bosnian archaeologist who has strongly criticized the claims.

mythologyConnections: Possible links to Bosnian folklore about giants and ancient settlements.
Speculation about connection to ancient sun worship.

timeline.event: Semir Osmanagić begins promoting the Bosnian Pyramids claim.
Ongoing archaeological investigations and debates surrounding the site.
Continued promotion of the Bosnian Pyramids as a tourist attraction.
